  <bead id="fizeau-b5757624">
    <title>routing: bound TCP dial timeout to ~5s, not Go default ~30s</title>
    <description>
PROBLEM
A dead local provider endpoint costs ~30 seconds of wall-clock per call before Fizeau surfaces the dial error. The fizeau-8134d6db AC required dial-class failures to bail in under 5 seconds; the shipped implementation only short-circuits the *retry loop* (one attempt instead of four), but the *dial itself* still uses Go's default TCP connect timeout (~30s on Linux). Observed today: every bead routed to bragi:1234 / bragi:8020 / grendel:1234 / grendel:1235 / grendel:8000 took ~30s to fail with "dial tcp ... i/o timeout". With 14 beads in the ddx queue all routing to a single dead host, the drain spent ~7 minutes establishing that the host was dead — meeting fizeau-8134d6db's letter (single attempt) while missing its spirit (under 5s).

ROOT CAUSE
internal/core/loop.go:643 (IsDialError short-circuit) is correctly wired. The retry-loop layer fails fast.

What is NOT bounded: the underlying http.Transport's DialContext. internal/provider/openai/openai.go and internal/sdk/openaicompat construct openaicompat.Config without overriding the HTTP client's transport. Go's net/http default Transport has no explicit DialContext, so it uses net.Dialer{} with Timeout=0 — which on Linux waits for the kernel's full TCP SYN retransmit timeout (≈21s + RTO backoff = 30s observed). For a known-local endpoint that the operator just unplugged, 30s is pure waste.

PROPOSED FIX
- Configure an explicit net.Dialer with Timeout=5s (or ServiceOptions-driven, defaulting to 5s) in the openaicompat client construction path.
- Wrap http.Transport.DialContext so the connect phase is bounded independent of the request streaming timeout.
- Keep the request-streaming timeout (Timeout field) separate — large requests legitimately take minutes once connected.
- Expose ConnectTimeout in openaicompat.Config so callers (DDx, benchmarks) can tune for high-latency networks if needed; default 5s.

NON-SCOPE
- Do not change the retry-loop short-circuit (already correct).
- Do not change in-flight 5xx retry behavior.
- Do not bound the request body / streaming timeout in this bead.

DEPS
None.
    </description>
    <acceptance>
1. TestOpenAIClient_DialTimeoutDefault5s asserts that the openaicompat.NewClient default Transport has an explicit DialContext with timeout ≤5s.
2. TestOpenAIClient_DialTimeoutConfigurable asserts ConnectTimeout in openaicompat.Config overrides the default.
3. TestProviderDeadLocalHost_FailsUnder5s — integration test: aim at an unroutable RFC 5737 address (e.g., 192.0.2.1:1) and assert the provider call returns within 5.5s with a dial error. (No process listening means kernel-level connect timeout exercises the bounded dial.)
4. go test ./... -count=1 passes.
5. CHANGELOG.md notes the new ConnectTimeout default + tunable.
    </acceptance>
    <labels>area:routing, area:provider, kind:bug</labels>

  <bead id="fizeau-59697e9d">
    <title>Async refresh scheduler and harnessByName helper</title>
    <description>
Source manifest: .ddx/beads-harness-interface-refactor.yaml
Source bead ID: BEAD-HARNESS-IF-04
Governing plan: docs/helix/02-design/plan-2026-05-14-harness-interface-refactor.md

See the manifest and the plan for full context, touch-point inventory, and sub-sequence ordering.
    </description>
    <acceptance>
1. Service struct gains a harnessByName(name string) harnesses.Harness method consulting the registered-harnesses field
2. service_refresh_scheduler.go owns a debounced ticker per registered QuotaHarness firing at QuotaFreshness()/2 and per AccountHarness whose AccountFreshness() differs from quota
3. Scheduler discovers harnesses by iterating service.harnessByName over the registry and type-asserting to QuotaHarness / AccountHarness independently
4. Scheduler reads QuotaStatus before each potential refresh to obtain CapturedAt; on cache-cold (State=QuotaUnavailable) the scheduler always calls RefreshQuota
5. Scheduler skips a tick when last-known QuotaStatus.CapturedAt is within QuotaFreshness() of now (cache-freshness dedup is the scheduler's job, not RefreshQuota's)
6. Startup refresh kickoff fires once on scheduler construction for every registered QuotaHarness / AccountHarness with a cache-cold state, per the cache-cold path in primary-harness-capability-baseline.md
7. Fake-clock tests prove debounce cadence, single-flight respect via cache lock, startup kickoff, and clean ctx cancel
8. Per-harness Refresh*Async helpers remain in place for now (deleted in each harness's e-step)
    </acceptance>
    <labels>area:harness, kind:refactor, spec:CONTRACT-004</labels>

  </bead>

    <acceptance>
1. claude.Runner implements QuotaStatus, RefreshQuota, QuotaFreshness, SupportedLimitIDs, AccountStatus, RefreshAccount, AccountFreshness, DefaultModelSnapshot, ResolveModelAlias, SupportedAliases
2. RefreshQuota probes unconditionally and uses the existing cache lock for single-flight (queued callers consume in-flight result)
3. Routing-preference mapping helper translates today's PreferClaude+freshness into RoutingPreference
4. package claude doc comment (doc.go or runner.go top) mirrors SupportedLimitIDs() and SupportedAliases() return values
5. harnesstest.RunHarnessConformance, RunQuotaHarnessConformance, RunAccountHarnessConformance, RunModelDiscoveryHarnessConformance all pass against &claude.Runner{}
6. Old per-harness cache I/O functions still exported and unchanged; both surfaces coexist
    </acceptance>

    </description>
    <acceptance>
1. claude.Runner implements QuotaStatus, RefreshQuota, QuotaFreshness, SupportedLimitIDs, AccountStatus, RefreshAccount, AccountFreshness, DefaultModelSnapshot, ResolveModelAlias, SupportedAliases
2. RefreshQuota probes unconditionally and uses the existing cache lock for single-flight (queued callers consume in-flight result)
3. Routing-preference mapping helper translates today's PreferClaude+freshness into RoutingPreference
4. package claude doc comment (doc.go or runner.go top) mirrors SupportedLimitIDs() and SupportedAliases() return values
5. harnesstest.RunHarnessConformance, RunQuotaHarnessConformance, RunAccountHarnessConformance, RunModelDiscoveryHarnessConformance all pass against &claude.Runner{}
6. Old per-harness cache I/O functions still exported and unchanged; both surfaces coexist
    </acceptance>

- The review is a gate, not an escape hatch — meet every AC in this pass.
- Address every BLOCKING `<review-findings>` item; do not declare `no_changes` with blocking findings open.

## Constraints

- Work only inside this execution worktree.
- Keep `.ddx/executions/` intact — DDx uses it as execution evidence.
- **Never run `ddx init`** — the workspace is initialized; running it corrupts the bead queue.
- Do not modify files outside the bead's named scope.
- Do not rewrite CLAUDE.md, AGENTS.md, or other project-instructions files unless the bead asks.

## When the work is done

After the commit succeeds and every AC is verified, stop. Return control to the orchestrator — do not keep exploring or testing.
  </instructions>

Covers beads (work items), the queue, executions, agents, harnesses, personas, reviews, spec-id. Use when the user says \"do work\", \"drain the queue\", \"run the next bead\", \"execute a bead\", \"review this\", \"check against spec\", \"what's on the queue\", \"what's ready\", \"create a bead\", \"file this as work\", \"run an agent\", \"dispatch\", \"use a persona\", \"how am I doing\", \"ddx doctor\", or mentions any ddx CLI command.\n---\n\n# DDx\n\nDDx (Document-Driven Development eXperience) is a CLI platform for\ndocument-driven development. It ships a bead tracker (portable work\nitems with acceptance criteria), an agent service (harness dispatch\nwith profile-first routing), a persona system (bindable AI\npersonalities), a library registry (plugins with prompts, templates,\npersonas), and git-aware synchronization. This skill makes any\nskills-compatible coding agent (Claude Code, OpenAI Codex, Gemini\nCLI, etc.) understand and operate the DDx surface correctly.\n\n## How this skill works\n\nThe skill body you're reading is an **overview** plus an **intent\nrouter**. The real domain guidance lives in `reference/*.md` files.\n\n**Directive: before responding to any DDx-related request, read the\nmatching reference file from the router table below. The router is\nnot optional — your answer must be grounded in the reference file's\nguidance, not this overview alone.**\n\n## Vocabulary\n\nSingle source of truth for DDx terms. Every reference file uses these\nexact definitions.\n\n- **Bead** — a portable work item (task, bug, epic, chore) with\n  metadata, dependencies, and acceptance criteria. `ddx bead create`.\n- **Queue** — the set of open beads. The *ready queue* is the subset\n  with all dependencies closed. `ddx bead ready`, `ddx bead blocked`.\n- **Ready** — a bead whose dependencies are all closed and which is\n  eligible to be picked up next. `ddx bead ready`.\n- **Blocked** — a bead with at least one unclosed dependency.\n  `ddx bead blocked`.\n- **Claim** — mark a bead as in-progress by an agent (concurrent-write\n  protection). `ddx bead update <id> --claim`.\n- **Close** — mark a bead as done, with evidence (session, commit\n  SHA). `ddx bead close <id>`. Beads only close on execution outcomes\n  `success` or `already_satisfied`.\n- **Work** — drain the bead queue. `ddx work` (alias for\n  `ddx agent execute-loop`); see `reference/work.md`.\n- **Execute-bead** — the primitive: run one agent on one bead in an\n  isolated worktree. `ddx agent execute-bead <id>`. The loop wraps\n  this.\n- **Execute-loop** — the queue-drain orchestrator; picks ready beads\n  one at a time, calls execute-bead, closes on success, unclaims on\n  failure. `ddx agent execute-loop` (or `ddx work`).\n- **Execution** — a generic DDx execution run (FEAT-010); broader\n  than execute-bead. Includes execution definitions, execution\n  records, and execution evidence under `.ddx/executions/<id>/`.\n- **Agent** — an AI coding agent (Claude, Codex, Gemini, etc.)\n  invoked via a harness. Not a subagent (harness-specific — see\n  below).\n- **Harness** — the abstraction DDx uses to dispatch an agent\n  (subprocess for claude/codex/gemini; embedded for the built-in\n  `agent` harness). `ddx agent list`, `ddx agent capabilities`.\n- **Persona** — a Markdown file (YAML frontmatter + body) that\n  defines an AI personality. DDx injects the body as a system-prompt\n  addendum to `ddx agent run`. `ddx persona list/show/bind`.\n- **Role** — an abstract function (e.g., `code-reviewer`,\n  `test-engineer`) a workflow can reference. Projects bind roles to\n  personas.\n- **Binding** — a project-specific `role: persona` map in\n  `.ddx/config.yaml` under `persona_bindings`.\n- **Profile** — intent-based routing policy for `ddx agent run`:\n  `cheap` (low-cost), `fast` (low-latency), `smart` (balanced\n  default). `--profile cheap|fast|smart`.\n- **Plugin** — a self-contained extension installed to\n  `.ddx/plugins/<name>/`. The default `ddx` plugin (personas,\n  prompts, patterns, templates) is auto-installed by `ddx init`.\n  `ddx install <name>`.\n- **Skill** — an agentskills.io-standard directory (SKILL.md +\n  optional `reference/`, `evals/`, `scripts/`). This `ddx` skill is\n  the one DDx ships. Plugins can ship additional skills.\n- **Subagent** — a harness-local concept for running a prompt in an\n  isolated context (Claude Code's `.claude/agents/` + `context:\n  fork`; Codex's `agents/`; others differ). DDx's `ddx agent run`\n  (especially with `--quorum`) is the portable primitive; how a\n  harness wraps that in a subagent is harness business. This skill\n  does not take a position on subagent orchestration.\n- **Update** — refresh plugin/toolkit content to a newer version.\n  `ddx update [<plugin>]`.\n- **Upgrade** — replace the DDx binary with a newer release.\n  `ddx upgrade`.\n- **Review** — two distinct concepts. **Bead review**\n  (`ddx bead review <id>`) grades a completed bead against its\n  acceptance criteria. **Quorum review** (`ddx agent run\n  --quorum=<policy> --harnesses=<list>`) dispatches a review prompt\n  across multiple harnesses and aggregates. See `reference/review.md`.\n- **Governing artifact** — the document that authorizes a bead's\n  work: a FEAT-\\*, SD-\\*, TD-\\*, or ADR-\\* under `docs/`. Referenced\n  via `spec-id`.\n- **Spec-id** — the `spec-id: <ID>` custom field on a bead pointing\n  at its governing artifact.\n\n## Intent router\n\nBefore responding, read the matching file.\n\n| User says / asks about | Read this file |\n|---|---|\n| write/plan work, \"create a bead\", \"file this as work\", bead metadata, acceptance criteria, dependencies | `reference/beads.md` |\n| \"do work\", \"drain the queue\", \"run the next bead\", \"execute a bead\", \"run work\", verify-and-close | `reference/work.md` |\n| \"review this\", \"check against spec\", bead review, quorum review, code review, adversarial check | `reference/review.md` |\n| \"run an agent\", \"dispatch\", harnesses, profiles, models, effort, \"use a persona\", role bindings | `reference/agents.md` |\n| \"what's on the queue\", \"what's ready\", \"how am I doing\", health check, \"ddx doctor\", sync status | `reference/status.md` |\n\nIf the intent spans multiple files (e.g., \"create a bead and then\nrun it\"), read beads.md first, then work.md. If no match, ask the\nuser which concept they mean rather than guessing.\n\n## Top-level policy reminders\n\nThese apply across all DDx operations. Do not restate them in every\nreference file; do not violate them.\n\n- **Never edit `.ddx/beads.jsonl` directly.** All tracker changes go\n  through `ddx bead create/update/close/dep`. Direct edits corrupt\n  bead history and cannot be audited.\n- **Tracker changes are commit-worthy.** After `ddx bead create`,\n  `update`, `dep add/remove`, or `close`, commit the resulting\n  `.ddx/beads.jsonl` change — either as a tracker-only commit or\n  folded into the same commit as related implementation changes.\n- **Preserve execute-bead commit history.** Branches containing\n  `ddx agent execute-bead` commits carry an audit trail. **Never\n  squash, rebase, filter, or amend** these commits. Use only\n  `git merge --ff-only` or `git merge --no-ff` when merging.\n  `gh pr merge --squash` and `--rebase` are forbidden on these\n  branches.\n- **Work in worktrees for parallel agents.** Use `wt switch -c\n  <branch>` (worktrunk) or equivalent to give each concurrent agent\n  its own isolated checkout. Execute-bead does this automatically;\n  manual parallel work should too.\n- **Profile-first agent dispatch.** Default to\n  `ddx agent run --profile smart` (or `cheap`/`fast` for specific\n  intents). Only override with `--harness`/`--model`/`--effort`\n  when pinning for a controlled test or known provider bug.\n\n## Links out\n\n- Full CLI reference: `ddx --help`, `ddx <subcommand> --help`.\n- Governing feature specs: see `FEAT-*` documents under your\n  project's `docs/` tree — especially the CLI, beads, agent-service,\n  executions, and skills features.\n- Personas README: shipped by the default `ddx` plugin at\n  `.ddx/plugins/ddx/personas/README.md`.\n- Open standard this skill conforms to:\n  [agentskills.io](https://agentskills.io).\n","exit_code":0,"status":"completed"}}

{"type":"item.completed","item":{"id":"item_2","type":"command_execution","command":"/bin/zsh -lc \"sed -n '1,220p' /Users/erik/Projects/agent/.agents/skills/ddx/reference/review.md\"","aggregated_output":"# Review — Bead Review and Quorum Review\n\nDDx has two distinct review concepts. They are **not interchangeable**.\n\n| Concept | Command | What it does |\n|---|---|---|\n| **Bead review** | `ddx bead review <id>` | Grades a bead's implementation against its declared acceptance criteria. Per-AC verdict with evidence. |\n| **Quorum review** | `ddx agent run --quorum=<policy> --harnesses=<list>` | Dispatches a review prompt across multiple harnesses and aggregates the verdicts. Used for high-stakes or adversarial checks. |\n\n## Bead review\n\n```bash\nddx bead review <bead-id>\n```\n\nGenerates a review-ready prompt that includes:\n\n- Bead title, description, acceptance criteria (verbatim).\n- Governing artifact content (follows `spec-id`).\n- The diff since the bead's base commit (execution evidence commits\n  excluded — see the related bug ddx-39e27896 for exclusion\n  pathspec).\n- Review instructions: output contract, verdict taxonomy.\n\nThe reviewer (whoever receives the prompt — a human, a harness via\n`ddx agent run`, or a quorum) produces a structured verdict:\n\n```\n## Bead review: ddx-<id>\n\n### Verdict: APPROVE | REQUEST_CHANGES | BLOCK\n\n### Per-criterion\n\n1. [AC text]\n   Verdict: APPROVE | REQUEST_CHANGES | BLOCK\n   Evidence: <file:line, test output, diff hunk, or explicit \"not verifiable\">\n\n2. [AC text]\n   ...\n\n### Summary\n<1-3 sentences of overall context>\n\n### Findings\n<detailed issues, if any; omit when verdict is APPROVE with no findings>\n```\n\nThe review output can be stored as bead evidence:\n\n```bash\nddx bead evidence add <id> --type review --body <path-to-review.md>\n```\n\n## Quorum review\n\n```bash\nddx agent run --quorum=majority --harnesses=claude,codex,gemini \\\n  --prompt review-prompt.md\n```\n\n`--quorum=<policy>` values:\n\n- `majority` — at least ⌈N/2⌉ harnesses must agree on the verdict.\n- `unanimous` — all harnesses must agree.\n- `any` — pass if any harness APPROVES (rarely useful; adversarial\n  checks should require stronger consensus).\n\nUse quorum when:\n\n- The change is high-stakes (migrations, security surface, API\n  contracts, auth).\n- The review surface is controversial and one harness's opinion\n  isn't enough.\n- You want adversarial pressure against a single-model blindspot\n  (different model families have different failure modes).\n\nThe aggregated output shows each harness's verdict side-by-side plus\nthe aggregate decision. Disagreements are surfaced, not hidden.\n\n## Required evidence format\n\nBoth review kinds share an evidence contract: **no verdict without\nevidence.**\n\n- \"APPROVE\" with no `Evidence:` lines → reject as malformed.\n- \"REQUEST_CHANGES\" without pointing at the change needed → reject.\n- \"BLOCK\" without a reason → reject (see the related bug\n  ddx-39e27896 about `review-malfunction` retries).\n\nEvidence takes one of these forms:\n\n1. **File:line reference**: `cli/internal/bead/store.go:142`\n2. **Test output**: `FAIL: TestBeadClose_UnclaimsOnNonSuccess (0.01s)`\n3. **Diff hunk**: a short quote from the diff showing the offending\n   or approved change.\n4. **Explicit \"not verifiable\"**: the AC is written in a way that\n   cannot be mechanically checked; flag this as a bead-authoring\n   issue rather than silently approving.\n\n## Anti-patterns\n\n- **Hallucinated approval.** \"Looks good to me!\" with no per-AC\n  grading. The review is worthless.\n- **Reviewing the prompt instead of the diff.** The reviewer evaluated\n  \"what you intended\" not \"what you actually changed.\" Verdict must\n  reference the diff or the resulting code.\n- **Verdict without evidence.** Every verdict must have at least one\n  evidence line; APPROVE is no exception (evidence can be \"AC met:\n  `go test ./foo/... passes` output attached\").\n- **Silencing disagreement in quorum.** If two harnesses disagree,\n  don't collapse to a single \"decision.\" Surface the disagreement\n  in the output and let the human decide.\n- **Quorum for routine work.** Single-harness bead review is fine\n  for most beads. Reserve quorum for the cases that justify the\n  cost.\n\n## Subagents: not this skill's business\n\nSome harnesses can run a quorum in forked/isolated subagent\ncontexts; each harness implements this differently. This skill\ndoesn't specify how isolation is achieved —\n`ddx agent run --quorum=<policy> --harnesses=<list>` is the\nportable invocation, and the harness decides whether to fork,\nspawn, or run inline.\n\n## CLI reference\n\n```bash\n# Bead review (single-reviewer AC grading)\nddx bead review <id>                           # generate review prompt\nddx bead review <id> --execute --harness claude  # dispatch and grade\nddx bead evidence add <id> --type review --body r.md  # store review\n\n# Quorum review (multi-harness)\nddx agent run --quorum=majority \\\n  --harnesses=claude,codex \\\n  --prompt review.md\n\nddx agent run --quorum=unanimous \\\n  --harnesses=claude,codex,gemini \\\n  --prompt review.md\n```\n\nFull flag list: `ddx bead review --help`, `ddx agent run --help`.\n","exit_code":0,"status":"completed"}}

CHANGELOG.md entry describing the new accessor.\",\"claimed-at\":\"2026-04-18T01:40:17Z\",\"claimed-machine\":\"eitri\",\"claimed-pid\":\"1635356\",\"created_at\":\"2026-04-18T01:22:17.516353249Z\",\"description\":\"## Context\\n\\nv0.3.11 introduced ProviderConfig.Flavor (lmstudio | omlx | openrouter | ollama) with probe-based autodetection when the field is absent. The probe hits /v1/models/status and /api/v0/models to figure out which flavor the endpoint speaks.\\n\\nDownstream consumers (DDx at DocumentDrivenDX/ddx, specifically the autorouting plan under ddx-2d974641 and the capability-gating bead ddx-4817edfd) need to know which flavor the library decided on for each configured provider — so their own routing/scoring/diagnostics can say 'this provider is omlx-flavor, so tool schemas go through auto-translation' without repeating the probe.\\n\\nToday this is not exposed via the public API. Downstream has two choices:\\n1. Re-run the same probe independently. Wastes the library's work and can produce different answers if servers flap.\\n2. Hard-code assumptions based on port numbers or URL patterns. Fragile — exactly the heuristic v0.3.11 replaced.\\n\\n## Ask\\n\\nExpose a getter on the resolved Provider (post-Load, post-detect) that returns the effective flavor:\\n\\n    // DetectedFlavor returns the flavor the runtime is using for this provider.\\n    // If ProviderConfig.Flavor was set explicitly, returns that value. Otherwise\\n    // returns the auto-detected flavor from the startup probe. Empty string when\\n    // the probe was inconclusive or the provider is not a supported flavor-family.\\n    func (p *Provider) DetectedFlavor() string\\n\\nCache the probe result so DetectedFlavor is a cheap lookup, not a fresh HTTP call.\\n\\n## Use case\\n\\nDDx uses this to:\\n- Tag routing events with the effective flavor so debugging is self-contained in execution evidence.\\n- Feed capability gating (supports_tools, supports_structured_output) off the flavor in scoring.\\n- Generate human-readable 'ddx agent check --verbose' output showing each provider's flavor.\\n\\n## Out of scope\\n\\n- Exposing the RAW probe results (HTTP responses). Only the distilled flavor.\\n- Making the flavor mutable at runtime. Providers are loaded + detected once per process.\",\"execute-loop-heartbeat-at\":\"2026-04-18T01:40:17.954331827Z\",\"id\":\"agent-92f0f324\",\"issue_type\":\"task\",\"labels\":[\"agent\",\"kind:enhancement\",\"area:provider\",\"area:api\"],\"notes\":\"Fresh-eyes review 2026-04-18: Provider already has a flavor-adjacent field — providerSystem (URL-heuristic from openAIIdentity, stored at New() time). It's used on 4 hot paths: AttemptMetadata.ProviderSystem, openRouterCostAttribution, ProviderIdentity(), and streaming attempt metadata. Those fire every response and cannot afford a network probe. DetectedFlavor() must layer on top: return explicit Config.Flavor if set, otherwise cached probe result via sync.Once, otherwise fall back to providerSystem. Do NOT migrate the 4 hot callsites off providerSystem in this bead — that's a telemetry contract change (CONTRACT-001) and scope creep. Docstring must explicitly document the two-path layering: DetectedFlavor() for pre-dispatch gating (probe-confirmed); ProviderIdentity() for per-response telemetry (eager, non-blocking).\",\"owner\":\"erik\",\"priority\":2,\"status\":\"closed\",\"title\":\"expose Provider.DetectedFlavor() so downstream tools can introspect probe result without re-probing\",\"updated_at\":\"2026-04-18T01:57:30.690987048Z\"}\nHEAD:.ddx/beads.jsonl:147:{\"acceptance\":\"- [ ] DdxAgent.TailSessionLog(ctx, sessionID) returns \\u003c-chan Event closed when session completes\\n- [ ] Subscriber attached mid-execution receives all subsequent events\\n- [ ] Subscriber attached after completion receives the final event then channel closes\\n- [ ] Multiple subscribers on the same sessionID each receive full streams\",\"closing_commit_sha\":\"bbb01c24cf5eb15e2ae11c37c207bf79feb2aac6\",\"created_at\":\"2026-04-18T21:24:56.709043765Z\",\"description\":\"Implement DdxAgent.TailSessionLog(ctx, sessionID) per CONTRACT-001. The accessor reads from the cached probe result — no HTTP call on every invocation.\\n\\n3. If ProviderConfig.Flavor was set explicitly, DetectedFlavor() returns that value unchanged (caller-set wins over probe).\\n\\n4. Unit test: probe returns 'omlx' signal -\\u003e Provider.DetectedFlavor() returns 'omlx'. ProviderConfig.Flavor='lmstudio' + probe says 'omlx' -\\u003e DetectedFlavor() returns 'lmstudio'.\\n\\n5. CHANGELOG.md entry describing the new accessor.\",\"claimed-at\":\"2026-04-18T01:40:17Z\",\"claimed-machine\":\"eitri\",\"claimed-pid\":\"1635356\",\"created_at\":\"2026-04-18T01:22:17.516353249Z\",\"description\":\"## Context\\n\\nv0.3.11 introduced ProviderConfig.Flavor (lmstudio | omlx | openrouter | ollama) with probe-based autodetection when the field is absent. The probe hits /v1/models/status and /api/v0/models to figure out which flavor the endpoint speaks.\\n\\nDownstream consumers (DDx at DocumentDrivenDX/ddx, specifically the autorouting plan under ddx-2d974641 and the capability-gating bead ddx-4817edfd) need to know which flavor the library decided on for each configured provider — so their own routing/scoring/diagnostics can say 'this provider is omlx-flavor, so tool schemas go through auto-translation' without repeating the probe.\\n\\nToday this is not exposed via the public API. Downstream has two choices:\\n1. Re-run the same probe independently. Wastes the library's work and can produce different answers if servers flap.\\n2. Hard-code assumptions based on port numbers or URL patterns. Fragile — exactly the heuristic v0.3.11 replaced.\\n\\n## Ask\\n\\nExpose a getter on the resolved Provider (post-Load, post-detect) that returns the effective flavor:\\n\\n    // DetectedFlavor returns the flavor the runtime is using for this provider.\\n    // If ProviderConfig.Flavor was set explicitly, returns that value. Otherwise\\n    // returns the auto-detected flavor from the startup probe. Empty string when\\n    // the probe was inconclusive or the provider is not a supported flavor-family.\\n    func (p *Provider) DetectedFlavor() string\\n\\nCache the probe result so DetectedFlavor is a cheap lookup, not a fresh HTTP call.\\n\\n## Use case\\n\\nDDx uses this to:\\n- Tag routing events with the effective flavor so debugging is self-contained in execution evidence.\\n- Feed capability gating (supports_tools, supports_structured_output) off the flavor in scoring.\\n- Generate human-readable 'ddx agent check --verbose' output showing each provider's flavor.\\n\\n## Out of scope\\n\\n- Exposing the RAW probe results (HTTP responses). Only the distilled flavor.\\n- Making the flavor mutable at runtime. Providers are loaded + detected once per process.\",\"execute-loop-heartbeat-at\":\"2026-04-18T01:40:17.954331827Z\",\"id\":\"agent-92f0f324\",\"issue_type\":\"task\",\"labels\":[\"agent\",\"kind:enhancement\",\"area:provider\",\"area:api\"],\"notes\":\"Fresh-eyes review 2026-04-18: Provider already has a flavor-adjacent field — providerSystem (URL-heuristic from openAIIdentity, stored at New() time). It's used on 4 hot paths: AttemptMetadata.ProviderSystem, openRouterCostAttribution, ProviderIdentity(), and streaming attempt metadata. Those fire every response and cannot afford a network probe. DetectedFlavor() must layer on top: return explicit Config.Flavor if set, otherwise cached probe result via sync.Once, otherwise fall back to providerSystem. Do NOT migrate the 4 hot callsites off providerSystem in this bead — that's a telemetry contract change (CONTRACT-001) and scope creep. Docstring must explicitly document the two-path layering: DetectedFlavor() for pre-dispatch gating (probe-confirmed); ProviderIdentity() for per-response telemetry (eager, non-blocking).\",\"owner\":\"erik\",\"priority\":2,\"status\":\"closed\",\"title\":\"expose Provider.DetectedFlavor() so downstream tools can introspect probe result without re-probing\",\"updated_at\":\"2026-04-18T01:57:30.690987048Z\"}\nHEAD:.ddx/beads.jsonl:147:{\"acceptance\":\"- [ ] DdxAgent.TailSessionLog(ctx, sessionID) returns \\u003c-chan Event closed when session completes\\n- [ ] Subscriber attached mid-execution receives all subsequent events\\n- [ ] Subscriber attached after completion receives the final event then channel closes\\n- [ ] Multiple subscribers on the same sessionID each receive full streams\",\"closing_commit_sha\":\"bbb01c24cf5eb15e2ae11c37c207bf79feb2aac6\",\"created_at\":\"2026-04-18T21:24:56.709043765Z\",\"description\":\"Implement DdxAgent.TailSessionLog(ctx, sessionID) per CONTRACT-001. When a later streamed delta becomes ready while an earlier callback is still running, this subtracts both observer latency and real provider generation time that elapsed during that callback. In the shipped providers, ChatStream uses a single-slot channel, so later deltas can queue behind the consumer and then be back-dated when read. That makes ddx.timing.generation_ms and delta event timestamps too small or even flat across distinct arrivals, which violates CONTRACT-001's requirement that timing reflect the streamed output window rather than an adjusted observer schedule.\\nSuggested fix: Capture an arrival timestamp at the producer or carry it on StreamDelta so consumeStream can use actual delta arrival times. At minimum, add a regression test that combines callback sleep with real delayBetween timing and verifies generation_ms still includes the provider delay that elapsed while the callback was running.\",\"id\":\"hx-1fcb3c84\",\"issue_type\":\"task\",\"labels\":[\"helix\",\"phase:build\",\"review-finding\"],\"owner\":\"erik\",\"priority\":0,\"spec-id\":\"stream_consume.go\",\"status\":\"closed\",\"title\":\"correctness: callback-delay compensation undercounts real stream generation\",\"updated_at\":\"2026-04-10T01:45:22.728662476Z\"}\nHEAD:.ddx/beads.jsonl:285:{\"acceptance\":\"A session whose chat spans all have known cost preserves a root invoke_agent ddx.cost.amount equal to the sum of child costs even when provenance differs across turns; the root span only emits ddx.cost.source=unknown when at least one contributing chat span has unknown cost; automated tests cover mixed provider-reported/configured cost rollups.\",\"claimed-at\":\"2026-04-10T04:17:15Z\",\"claimed-machine\":\"eitri\",\"claimed-pid\":\"517258\",\"closing_commit_sha\":\"c083df5f46fe37557b8feedf66af32013a0a9e35\",\"created_at\":\"2026-04-10T04:02:07.463686182Z\",\"description\":\"Review finding from fresh-eyes review.\\nFile: loop.go:289\\nCategory: correctness\\nSeverity: medium\\nDescription: The new root-span cost rollup logic marks the invoke_agent span as unknown whenever known child chat spans disagree on ddx.cost.source, currency, or pricing_ref. That drops a valid total for mixed but fully known sessions, which violates CONTRACT-001's rule that the root cost only becomes unknown when a contributing chat span has unknown cost. Reproduced against the current code by running a two-turn session with a provider-reported first turn and an exact configured-cost second turn: result.CostUSD=0.03 while the root span still emits ddx.cost.source=unknown.\\nSuggested fix: Roll up root ddx.cost.amount whenever every contributing chat span has known cost, and only force ddx.cost.source=unknown when a contributing span is actually unknown. If metadata such as pricing_ref or source cannot be represented cleanly at the root, omit or summarize those fields rather than discarding the known total. But the contract never defines what ddx.cost.source, ddx.cost.currency, and ddx.cost.pricing_ref should look like when all child chat costs are known yet their provenance differs across turns, such as one provider-reported turn plus one configured-cost turn. That makes the required root shape ambiguous: ddx.cost.source is required when amount is known, but the allowed enum only models single-attempt provenance. The current implementation fell into this gap and downgraded a known total to unknown when provenance metadata differed.\\nSuggested fix: Amend the root-span cost-rollup section to specify a conformant representation for mixed known-cost provenance. Either add an explicit aggregate/root rollup source shape, or state that the root may still emit ddx.cost.amount and currency while omitting or summarizing provenance fields that cannot be represented cleanly. Add a contract-focused regression test or checklist entry covering mixed provider-reported/configured sessions.\",\"id\":\"hx-7853958b\",\"issue_type\":\"task\",\"labels\":[\"helix\",\"phase:build\",\"review-finding\"],\"owner\":\"erik\",\"priority\":0,\"spec-id\":\"docs/helix/02-design/contracts/CONTRACT-001-otel-telemetry-capture.md\",\"status\":\"closed\",\"title\":\"correctness: finding from fresh-eyes review.\\nFile: loop.go:175\\nCategory: correctness\\nSeverity: medium\\nDescription: Commit 4df0f7e moved `streamStart` earlier inside `consumeStream`, but `ddx.timing.first_token_ms` is still measured after the chat span starts. `Run` starts the `chat` span at loop.go:175, then emits `EventLLMRequest` before calling `consumeStream`; because callbacks are user-supplied and can block, the exported attribute can still under-report first-token latency by the callback time and therefore violate CONTRACT-001's requirement that the value measure from span start to first streamed token.\\nSuggested fix: Capture the chat span/request start timestamp in `Run` and thread it into `consumeStream` so `Attempt.Timing.FirstToken` is derived from the same timestamp that anchors the `chat` span. Add an end-to-end test where the request callback sleeps before streaming begins and assert the chat span's `ddx.timing.first_token_ms` includes that When any contributing attempt has unknown provenance, result.CostUSD becomes -1, so session.end now emits `cost_usd: -1` instead of omitting the amount. Consumers see one surface; the internals decide how to dispatch.\n    33\t\n    34\t## Interface\n    35\t\n    36\t```go\n    37\tpackage agentlib\n    38\t\n    39\timport (\n    40\t    \"context\"\n    41\t    \"io\"\n    42\t    \"time\"\n    43\t    \"encoding/json\"\n    44\t)\n    45\t\n    46\t// DdxAgent is the entire public Go surface of the ddx-agent module.\n    47\ttype DdxAgent interface {\n    48\t    // Execute runs an agent task in-process; emits Events on the channel until\n    49\t    // the task terminates (channel closes). The final event (type=final) carries\n    50\t    // status, usage, cost, session log path, optional message history, and\n    51\t    // routing_actual (the resolved fallback chain that fired).\n    52\t    Execute(ctx context.Context, req ExecuteRequest) (<-chan Event, error)\n    53\t\n    54\t    // TailSessionLog streams events from a previously-started or in-progress\n    55\t    // session by ID. Used by clients (DDx workers, UIs) to subscribe to a run\n    56\t    // started elsewhere — e.g., a server-managed worker that the CLI wants to\n    57\t    // follow. Multi-subscriber-safe.\n    58\t    TailSessionLog(ctx context.Context, sessionID string) (<-chan Event, error)\n    59\t\n    60\t    // ListHarnesses returns metadata for every registered harness (native and\n    61\t    // subprocess). The returned RouteDecision can be passed\n    79\t    // back to Execute via ExecuteRequest.PreResolved to skip re-resolution\n    80\t    // (used by dry-run-then-execute flows).\n    81\t    ResolveRoute(ctx context.Context, req RouteRequest) (*RouteDecision, error)\n    82\t\n    83\t    // RouteStatus returns global routing state across all routes: cooldowns,\n    84\t    // recent decisions, observation-derived per-(provider,model) latency.\n    85\t    // Distinct from per-request ResolveRoute — this is the read-only operator\n    86\t    // dashboard view.\n    87\t    RouteStatus(ctx context.Context) (*RouteStatusReport, error)\n    88\t}\n    89\t\n    90\t// New constructs a DdxAgent. Internal packages such as\n   122\t// internal/modelcatalog import the leaf package, not root agent, to avoid\n   123\t// root-agent/internal-modelcatalog import cycles.\n   124\ttype Reasoning string\n   125\t\n   126\tconst (\n   127\t    ReasoningAuto   Reasoning = \"auto\"\n   128\t    ReasoningOff    Reasoning = \"off\"\n   129\t    ReasoningLow    Reasoning = \"low\"\n   130\t    ReasoningMedium Reasoning = \"medium\"\n   131\t    ReasoningHigh   Reasoning = \"high\"\n   132\t    ReasoningMinimal Reasoning = \"minimal\" // accepted only when advertised\n   133\t    ReasoningXHigh  Reasoning = \"xhigh\"    // normalizes x-high\n   134\t    ReasoningMax    Reasoning = \"max\"      // requires known model/provider max\n   135\t)\n   136\t\n   137\tfunc ReasoningTokens(n int) Reasoning\n   138\t\n   139\ttype ExecuteRequest struct {\n   140\t    Prompt       string  // required\n   141\t    SystemPrompt string  // optional; agent supplies a sane default if empty\n   142\t    Model        string  // optional; resolved via ResolveRoute if empty\n   143\t    Provider     string  // optional preference (soft); empty = router decides\n   144\t    Harness      string  // optional preference (hard); empty = router decides\n   145\t    ModelRef     string  // optional alias from the catalog: cheap/standard/smart/<custom>\n   146\t    Temperature  float32 // model sampling temperature; 0 = deterministic\n   147\t    Seed         int64   // sampling seed; 0 = unset/provider chooses\n   148\t    Reasoning    Reasoning // optional; auto|off|low|medium|high|minimal|xhigh|max|<tokens>\n   149\t    Permissions  string  // \"safe\" | \"supervised\" | \"unrestricted\"; default \"safe\"\n   150\t    WorkDir      string  // required when the chosen harness uses tools\n   151\t\n   152\t    // PreResolved bypasses ResolveRoute when the caller already has a decision\n   153\t    // (e.g., from a prior ResolveRoute call). Provider/Model/Harness fields\n   155\t    // above are ignored in this mode.\n   156\t    PreResolved *RouteDecision\n   157\t\n   158\t    // Three independent timeout knobs:\n   159\t    //   Timeout         — wall-clock cap; the request fails after this duration\n   160\t    //                     regardless of activity. 0 = no cap.\n   161\t    //   IdleTimeout     — streaming-quiet cap; the request fails after this\n   162\t    //                     duration of no events from the model. 0 = use harness\n   163\t    //                     default (typically 60s).\n   164\t    //   ProviderTimeout — per-HTTP-request cap to the provider; longer requests\n   165\t    //                     are retried per the harness's failover rules. 0 = use\n   166\t    //                     provider default.\n   167\t    Timeout         time.Duration\n   168\t    IdleTimeout     time.Duration\n   169\t    ProviderTimeout time.Duration\n   170\t\n   171\t    // Optional stall policy. The agent also derives an\n   173\t    // implicit MaxIterations ceiling from StallPolicy (typically 2× the\n   174\t    // ReadOnly limit) — caller does not configure MaxIterations directly.\n   175\t    StallPolicy *StallPolicy\n   176\t\n   177\t    // SessionLogDir overrides the default session-log directory for this\n   178\t    // request. Used by execute-bead to direct logs into a per-bundle evidence\n   179\t    // directory. Empty = use Options.ConfigPath-derived default.\n   180\t    SessionLogDir string\n   181\t\n   182\t    // Metadata is bidirectional: echoed back in every Event via Event.Metadata,\n   183\t    // Provider/Model/Harness fields above are ignored.\n\tPreResolved *RouteDecision\n\n\t// Three independent timeout knobs:\n\t//   Timeout         — wall-clock cap on the entire request.\n\t//   IdleTimeout     — streaming-quiet cap; per-stream gap.\n\t//   ProviderTimeout — per-HTTP-request cap to the provider.\n\tTimeout         time.Duration\n\tIdleTimeout     time.Duration\n\tProviderTimeout time.Duration\n\n\t// Optional stall policy. When non-nil agent enforces and ends with\n\t// Status=\"stalled\" if any limit hits. Default policy applies when nil.\n\tStallPolicy *StallPolicy\n\n\t// SessionLogDir overrides the default session-log directory for this\n\t// request (e.g. an execute-bead per-bundle evidence directory).\n\tSessionLogDir string\n\n\t// Metadata is bidirectional: echoed back in every Event AND stamped\n\t// onto every line of the session log so external consumers correlate.\n\tMetadata map[string]string\n}\n    ServiceExecuteRequest is the public ExecuteRequest type per CONTRACT-003.\n    See docs/helix/02-design/contracts/CONTRACT-003-ddx-agent-service.md\n    (§\"Public types\") for the canonical shape; this struct is its in-process\n    twin under the agent module.\n\ntype ServiceModelRouteConfig struct {\n\tStrategy   string                       // \"priority-round-robin\" | \"ordered-failover\" | \"smart\" | \"\"\n\tCandidates []ServiceRouteCandidateEntry // ordered candidate list\n}\n    ServiceModelRouteConfig carries the routing strategy and candidates for one\n    route.\n\ntype ServiceOptions struct {\n\tConfigPath string    // optional override; default $XDG_CONFIG_HOME/ddx-agent/config.yaml\n\tLogger     io.Writer // optional; agent writes structured session logs internally regardless\n\n\t// ServiceConfig, when non-nil, supplies provider and routing data for\n\t// ListProviders and HealthCheck. Used by tests that verify the right tools\n   406\t// land at the right harness given the request's permission level.\n   407\ttype ToolWiringHook func(harness string, toolNames []string)\n   408\t```\n   409\t\n   410\t## Reasoning contract\n   411\t\n   412\t`Reasoning` is the only preferred public control for model-side reasoning.\n   413\tConsumers do not set separate public thinking, effort, level, or budget fields.\n   414\tThe scalar accepts named values (`auto`, `off`, `low`, `medium`, `high`) and\n   415\tprovider/harness-supported extended values such as `minimal`, `xhigh` /\n   416\t`x-high`, and `max`. It also accepts numeric values through\n   417\t`ReasoningTokens(n)`, where `0` means explicit off and positive integers mean\n   418\tan explicit max reasoning-token budget or documented provider-equivalent\n   419\tnumeric value.\n   420\t\n   421\tNormalization is tri-state:\n   422\t\n   423\t- Empty means no caller preference.\n   424\t- `auto` means resolve model, catalog, or provider defaults.\n   425\t- `off`, `none`, `false`, and `0` mean explicit reasoning off.\n   426\t- Positive integers mean an explicit numeric request.\n   427\t\n   428\tDefault portable named-to-token budgets are `low=2048`, `medium=8192`, and\n   429\t`high=32768` only when a selected provider/model does not publish a more\n   430\tspecific map. Providers and subprocess harnesses may map resolved reasoning to\n   431\twire or CLI knobs named `reasoning`, `thinking`, `effort`, `variant`, or a\n   432\tnumeric budget. They may also drop auto/default reasoning controls for models\n   433\tthat do not support explicit reasoning control. Explicit unsupported values,\n   434\tunknown extended values, and over-limit numeric values fail clearly.\n   435\t\n   436\tCatalog tier defaults are part of route resolution: below-smart tiers\n   437\t(`cheap`, `fast`, `standard`, `code-economy`, and `code-medium`) default to\n   438\t`reasoning=off`, including local/economy Qwen targets. `smart` and `code-high`\n   439\tdefault to `reasoning=high`. Any explicit caller `Reasoning` value wins over\n   440\tthese defaults, including supported values above high such as `xhigh` or\n   441\t`max`, and numeric values.\n   442\t\n   443\t## Sampling contract\n   444\t\n   445\t`ExecuteRequest.Temperature` and `ExecuteRequest.Seed` are the portable\n   446\tsampling controls. `Temperature=0` requests deterministic sampling. `Seed=0`\n   447\tmeans unset and lets the provider choose. Native OpenAI-compatible providers\n   448\thonor both fields. Providers or subprocess harnesses that do not expose an\n   449\tequivalent seed control may ignore `Seed`; callers that require strict parity\n   450\tmust treat those runs as advisory/non-deterministic.\n   451\t\n   452\t## Bead Execution Policy\n   453\t\n   454\tDDx bead implementation should use a two-pass policy against this service:\n   455\ttry a cheap or standard profile with `reasoning=off` first, then escalate only\n   456\twhen the first pass produced evidence that a smarter model is likely to help.\n   457\tThe initial pass should use `ModelRef=cheap` or `ModelRef=standard` with an\n   458\texplicit `ReasoningOff` request so local/economy models do not spend\n   459\treasoning tokens by default.\n   460\t\n","exit_code":0,"status":"completed"}}

CONTRACT-003 amended to document the new state, error, and operator config knobs.","closing_commit_sha":"e01e404a7623abca214bb6f8586cae5ebcf4d1c8","created_at":"2026-05-02T18:05:43.402325681Z","description":"Auto-routing today handles per-request transient feedback (RecordRouteAttempt; CONTRACT-003 line 1413) but does NOT track per-provider quota state across requests. Result: when a provider hits its subscription/daily cap, every subsequent dispatch fails until operator notices. The 'point of auto-routing' is exactly this: take a quota-exhausted provider out of rotation, keep dispatching to others, periodically recheck.

## Multi-account model (downstream operator decision, locked)

Each API key = its own provider entry in ~/.config/agent/config.yaml (e.g., 'claude-acct-1', 'claude-acct-2'). Fizeau treats them independently. No new schema for multi-key-per-provider; reuse existing provider abstraction. Operator-friendly: rotate accounts by adding entries.

## Prediction model (locked)

Combine two signals:
1. Provider-supplied headers when available (Anthropic X-RateLimit-Remaining-* family, OpenAI rate-limit headers, OpenRouter equivalents)
2. Local burn-rate tracking — Fizeau maintains rolling per-provider token-usage window, compares against operator-configured per-provider budget (e.g., providers.claude-acct-1.daily_token_budget: 1_000_000)

When EITHER signal predicts exhaustion before the next reset, take the provider out proactively (don't wait for the actual quota error).

## Behavior on take-out

- Provider transitions to 'quota_exhausted' state with an estimated retry_after timestamp (from headers when available; from window-reset estimate from local tracking otherwise)
- Routing engine excludes quota_exhausted providers from candidate selection
- Other providers continue serving requests normally
- Periodic probe (lightweight HealthCheck) at provider's retry_after OR at a fallback interval (default 5 min) returns the provider to 'available' on success

## New error class: NoViableProviderForNow

Distinct from existing 'no-candidate' / 'config' errors. Returned when Execute has zero eligible providers solely because all are quota_exhausted (i.e., would otherwise be available). Includes:
  - retry_after timestamp (earliest expected provider recovery)
  - exhausted_providers []string (which providers are out)

DDx (downstream) consumes this to pause its drain loop and resume on retry_after.

## Out of scope

- Rate-limit handling (per-second / per-minute throttle via 429 + Retry-After header) — this stays in the per-request feedback path; provider stays in rotation. DDx handles per-bead retry. Distinct from this bead.
- Auto-account-creation / billing integration. Operator manages keys.
- Cost-budget enforcement (different concern; lives in DDx --max-cost flag)","events_attachment":"agent-63bf58a6/events.jsonl","execute-loop-no-changes-count":1,"id":"agent-63bf58a6","issue_type":"epic","labels":["area:fizeau","area:routing","kind:feature","downstream:ddx"],"notes":"Decomposed into children: fizeau-92b4b823 (state machine + NoViableProviderForNow error), fizeau-50b14297 (rate-limit header parsing for Anthropic/OpenAI/OpenRouter), fizeau-f2661619 (burn-rate tracker + daily_token_budget config), .execute-bead-wt-agent-63bf58a6-20260502T192505-092f5e0d-5208511b (periodic HealthCheck probe), .execute-bead-wt-agent-63bf58a6-20260502T192505-092f5e0d-78f47557 (CONTRACT-003 amendment). The model itself is functional, our harness needs a lot of work.'

This bead is the iteration loop, not a one-shot benchmark.

## Methodology

**Backing.** omlx Qwen3.6-27B-MLX-8bit on vidar (the local omlx host). NOT openrouter — the user's stated target is local omlx.

**Pi configuration.** Pi --list-models on the test machine doesn't currently include omlx. First step: ensure pi is configured to talk to omlx vidar (the user's target). This may require:
- Pi config file edit on the test machine (out of scope for direct manipulation per user directive 'just consume what pi tells us')
- OR a different test machine where pi already has omlx
- OR a pi command-line --provider flag that points at vidar

Capture the pi --list-models snapshot under the omlx-configured pi; that snapshot is the baseline evidence.

**Paired design.** Each task runs on BOTH harnesses with same model, same prompt:
- agent-omlx-vidar-qwen36 (harness=agent, provider=vidar-omlx, model=Qwen3.6-27B-MLX-8bit)
- pi-omlx-vidar-qwen36 (harness=pi, provider=<pi-internal-omlx>, model=Qwen3.6-27B-MLX-8bit)

**Reviewer pin.** Both arms use --review-harness codex --review-model gpt-5.5 (cannot be claude — confound).

**Sample size.** Minimum N=8 paired tasks; target N=12.

**Win condition.** Success rate ≥ pi's. Cost-per-success ≤ 1.2× pi's.

## Iteration loop (the actual work)

The baseline measurement is step zero. After it lands:

1. **Identify top-1 gap** — which lever moved the needle most against agent? Compaction policy, tool exposure, system prompt, or reasoning encoding?
2. **Tweak that lever** — file a small focused bead (e.g., 'compaction: tune Qwen-friendly summary trigger') and implement.
3. **Re-measure** — append a new dated parity row to the tracker (agent-f43d1ed2).
4. **Repeat** until agent matches or beats pi for 3 consecutive measurements.

The iteration beads are filed reactively as data surfaces, not upfront.

## Levers (per user signal)

- **Compaction.** internal/compaction/. May be too aggressive or too lax for Qwen's smaller context strategies.
- **Tools.** internal/tool/. Tool descriptions, parallelism, tool count. Qwen may be confused by tool sets that work fine for Claude/GPT.
- **System prompt.** internal/prompt/presets.go. Default and benchmark presets may be tuned for stronger models.
- **Reasoning levels.** Reasoning encoding through openai-compat to omlx. Qwen3.6 is a thinking-variant model; we may be sending the wrong wire shape.

## Deliverable (initial)

4. Aggregate metrics + 'winner: agent | pi | tie' line.
5. **Top-3 gaps** identified from the data — which lever moved the needle, ranked.

## What is done in this pass

1. Verified vidar OMLX endpoint reachability from this Linux worktree:
   `curl http://vidar:1235/v1/models` returns the OMLX catalog and includes

   `scripts/beadbench/manifest-v1.json:32-39` with the correct provider/model pin.

     `pi --list-models` →
       providers: anthropic, google-gemini-cli, openai-codex, openrouter
       qwen3.6   : openrouter qwen/qwen3.6-plus           (NOT the omlx vidar
                                                           Qwen3.6-27B target)
       no entry for vidar / omlx / lmstudio / openai-compatible / local

3. Cross-checked pi's reviewer-model availability for the AC#1-pinned
   `--review-harness codex --review-model gpt-5.5`:
     pi's openai-codex tier tops out at `gpt-5.4` (also `gpt-5.4-mini`,
     `gpt-5.3-codex`, etc.). `gpt-5.5` is not in pi's catalog.

## What is blocking

AC #2 ("Pi configuration verified: pi --list-models snapshot shows omlx is in
pi's reachable provider set") is **not satisfiable in this environment**. The
bead description anticipates this exact situation and lists three possible
unblockers:

  (a) Pi config-file edit on the test machine
        — explicitly out of scope per user directive
          "just consume what pi tells us"
  (b) A different test machine where pi already has omlx
        — execute-bead worktrees run on this Linux host; we cannot relocate
  (c) A pi command-line --provider flag pointing at vidar
        — pi has no openai-compatible / generic-http provider; the four
          providers listed above are the only options. There is no
          `--provider vidar-omlx` or equivalent.

Because pi cannot be made to call vidar OMLX, no `pi-omlx-vidar-qwen36` arm
can be exercised, no paired runs (AC #3, N≥8 in AC #4) can be produced,
no aggregate metrics (AC #5) or top-3 gaps ranked from pairwise data
(AC #6) can be filed honestly. Filing a one-arm "comparison" would defeat
the bead's purpose (driving the next iteration tweak from gap evidence).

A secondary blocker exists for the reviewer pin: `gpt-5.5` does not exist
in pi's catalog. Even if pi gained omlx, the manifest entry would have to
be reframed — likely against `gpt-5.4` (current codex top) or against the
codex CLI directly.

## What a follow-up attempt would need

To turn this bead green, one of the following must change first:

1. **Pi gains a generic openai-compatible provider** (e.g., `pi --provider
   openai-compat --base-url http://vidar:1235/v1 --model
   Qwen3.6-27B-MLX-8bit`). Once `pi --list-models` (or a
   `--provider openai-compat` invocation) can reach vidar, AC #2 becomes
   satisfiable and the paired runs become possible.

   This likely requires either (a) a pi version bump that adds
   openai-compatible support, or (b) a pi config-file edit registering
   vidar as a custom provider — the latter is explicitly out of scope per
   the user directive cited in the bead.

2. **OR the bead is reframed** to compare the agent's vidar OMLX arm
   against pi's nearest-available qwen3.6 (openrouter
   `qwen/qwen3.6-plus`). That changes the comparison from "same model,
   different harness" to "different model + different harness", which
   confounds the iteration signal the bead wants. Worth flagging to the
   user, but a reframe — not the work this bead asked for.

3. **AND the reviewer pin should be updated** from `gpt-5.5` to a model
   that exists in pi's catalog (e.g., `gpt-5.4` or `gpt-5.4-mini`), or the
   reviewer should be the codex CLI directly rather than via pi.

## Why no partial commit

A partial commit that adds the agent arm + a placeholder pi arm + an
empty-table research doc would (a) leave the manifest in an inconsistent
state (AC #1 cites an unreachable arm), (b) seed
`docs/research/AR-2026-04-26-agent-vs-pi-omlx-vidar-qwen36.md` with no
real pairwise data (defeating AC #3-#6), and (c) hide the actual blocker
behind apparent progress, slowing the next iteration. Per execute-bead
guidance, "a well-justified no_changes is better than a bad commit" —
returning the bead with this rationale lets the user choose whether to
unblock pi or reframe the comparison before the next attempt is queued.

Because pi cannot be made to call vidar OMLX, no `pi-omlx-vidar-qwen36` arm
can be exercised, no paired runs (AC #3, N≥8 in AC #4) can be produced,
no aggregate metrics (AC #5) or top-3 gaps ranked from pairwise data
(AC #6) can be filed honestly. Filing a one-arm "comparison" would defeat
the bead's purpose (driving the next iteration tweak from gap evidence).

A secondary blocker exists for the reviewer pin: `gpt-5.5` does not exist
in pi's catalog. Even if pi gained omlx, the manifest entry would have to
be reframed — likely against `gpt-5.4` (current codex top) or against the
codex CLI directly.

## What a follow-up attempt would need

To turn this bead green, one of the following must change first:

1. **Pi gains a generic openai-compatible provider** (e.g., `pi --provider
   openai-compat --base-url http://vidar:1235/v1 --model
   Qwen3.6-27B-MLX-8bit`). Once `pi --list-models` (or a
   `--provider openai-compat` invocation) can reach vidar, AC #2 becomes
   satisfiable and the paired runs become possible.

   This likely requires either (a) a pi version bump that adds
   openai-compatible support, or (b) a pi config-file edit registering
   vidar as a custom provider — the latter is explicitly out of scope per
   the user directive cited in the bead.

2. **OR the bead is reframed** to compare the agent's vidar OMLX arm
   against pi's nearest-available qwen3.6 (openrouter
   `qwen/qwen3.6-plus`). That changes the comparison from "same model,
   different harness" to "different model + different harness", which
   confounds the iteration signal the bead wants. Worth flagging to the
   user, but a reframe — not the work this bead asked for.

3. **AND the reviewer pin should be updated** from `gpt-5.5` to a model
   that exists in pi's catalog (e.g., `gpt-5.4` or `gpt-5.4-mini`), or the
   reviewer should be the codex CLI directly rather than via pi.

## Why no partial commit

A partial commit that adds the agent arm + a placeholder pi arm + an
empty-table research doc would (a) leave the manifest in an inconsistent
state (AC #1 cites an unreachable arm), (b) seed
`docs/research/AR-2026-04-26-agent-vs-pi-omlx-vidar-qwen36.md` with no
real pairwise data (defeating AC #3-#6), and (c) hide the actual blocker
behind apparent progress, slowing the next iteration. Per execute-bead
guidance, "a well-justified no_changes is better than a bad commit" —
returning the bead with this rationale lets the user choose whether to
unblock pi or reframe the comparison before the next attempt is queued.

## Evidence captured (for the next attempt)

- vidar OMLX reachable: `curl http://vidar:1235/v1/models` → 200, model
  catalog includes `Qwen3.6-27B-MLX-8bit`, `Qwen3.6-35B-A3B-4bit`,
  `Qwen3.6-35B-A3B-nvfp4`, `gpt-oss-20b-MXFP4-Q8`, etc.
- pi --list-models providers (full set): anthropic, google-gemini-cli,
  openai-codex, openrouter. No local / openai-compat / lmstudio / omlx.
- pi qwen3.6 catalog match: only `openrouter qwen/qwen3.6-plus`
  (1M ctx, 65.5K out, thinking=yes, images=yes) — not the local omlx
  target.
- pi codex top model: `openai-codex gpt-5.4` (272K ctx, 128K out,
  thinking=yes). `gpt-5.5` absent.

Attempt model: claude-opus-4-7
Outcome: no_changes — bead requires multi-hour benchmark execution that exceeds a single execute-bead pass.

== What is done in the worktree ==

Nothing committed. Investigation only. Verified:
  - pi --list-models exposes vidar provider with Qwen3.6-27B-MLX-8bit reachable (note in bead is current).
  - scripts/beadbench/manifest-v1.json already contains agent-vidar-omlx-qwen36-27b
    (the agent arm). It does NOT contain a pi arm pinned to vidar omlx; the
    existing pi-qwen36-27b arm has no provider field and pi internally
    selects bragi/grendel/vidar by its own routing.
  - scripts/beadbench/run_beadbench.py does NOT support --review-harness
    or --review-model flags; ddx agent execute-bead --help has no review
    flags. AC1's "with --review-harness codex --review-model gpt-5.5
    pinned" can only be expressed as manifest metadata (rationale field or
    a new `review` block); the runner would need code changes to actually
    pass them downstream. That code change is out of scope for this bead.
  - tasks in manifest-v1.json reference /Users/erik/Projects/{agent,ddx,
    helix,axon,niflheim} which do exist on this host. So the runner is
    not blocked on missing project_root.

== Why the bead cannot be completed in one pass ==

AC items 3-6 require executing N>=8 paired tasks (>=16 ddx execute-bead
invocations) through a remote local-inference model on vidar. Empirical
expectation per prior beadbench runs in scripts/beadbench/* and the 04-24
omlx-qwen-reasoning-sweep doc: each Qwen3.6-27B-MLX-8bit run takes 5-30+
minutes including verifier (cargo test / go test). A full 16-run paired
matrix is ~3-8 hours of wall time, plus per-task sandbox setup
(`git clone` of multi-GB repos like niflheim) and disk pressure under
/tmp. A single execute-bead Claude pass cannot keep an interactive
session alive for that duration without timing out, and doing the runs
in background and resuming would require splitting this work across
multiple execute-bead passes — exactly the iteration-loop structure the
bead description acknowledges ("filed reactively as data surfaces").

== What a follow-up attempt needs ==

Split this bead into:

  1. (manifest only) — add agent-omlx-vidar-qwen36 + pi-omlx-vidar-qwen36
     arms to scripts/beadbench/manifest-v1.json with reviewer metadata
     fields (review_harness=codex, review_model=gpt-5.5). Optionally
     extend run_beadbench.py to honor those fields if a review step is
     wired into ddx agent execute-bead. AC: manifest diff + go test.
     Tractable in one Claude pass.

  2. (pi config evidence) — capture pi --list-models snapshot and write
     docs/research/AR-2026-04-26-... methodology + pi-config-evidence
     sections only (sections 1-2 of the deliverable). AC: doc exists with
     methodology, pi snapshot, and a documented "next: run beadbench"
     command line. Tractable in one Claude pass.

  3. (the actual run) — invoke scripts/beadbench/run_beadbench.py against
     the new arms over a curated 8-task subset (mix of smoke + core
     tiers), append per-task pairwise rows to the doc, compute
     aggregates, and write the top-3-gaps section. Best run as a long
     background job, possibly outside execute-bead (the user runs it
     directly), then closed by a small follow-up bead that just lands the
     completed doc.

  4. (iteration beads) — file reactively per the description, one per
     identified top-1 gap, after #3 produces signal.

The bead description itself states this is "the iteration loop, not a
one-shot benchmark." Splitting per (1)-(4) above honors that structure
and keeps each child bead executable inside a single agent pass.

== Recommendation ==

Reopen this bead as an epic and file the four child beads above. Do not
escalate this bead's model — capacity is not the bottleneck; wall-clock
execution time of remote-model benchmark tasks is.

.ddx/executions/20260515T052443-8b266248/embedded/agent-codex-1778822691604098816.jsonl:14:{"type":"tool_result","sequence":13,"time":"2026-05-15T05:25:27.183941481Z","metadata":{"DDX_MODE":"bead_execution","attempt_id":"20260515T052443-8b266248","base_rev":"1dda3fe508ef5dc15e421e30795f0afe51180e6f","bead_id":"fizeau-4530e0be","bundle_path":".ddx/executions/20260515T052443-8b266248","prompt_file":".ddx/executions/20260515T052443-8b266248/prompt.md","prompt_sha":"354e7a8d150f7a41cfc366a239cdda9d363463433031ff9465a99b744fb5f91f","session_id":"eb-4ae86db1","worker_id":""},"data":{"id":"item_8","output":"# Work — Drain the Queue, Execute, Verify, Close

## Mode note

This reference covers **`bead_execution` mode**: explicit worker commands such as
`ddx work`, `ddx try <id>`, "execute bead `<id>`", or "start the worker".

If the user said "what should I work on next?", "what's blocking the queue?", or
"work the queue" without an explicit bead ID or `ddx work` command, they are in
**`interactive-steward`** mode — see `reference/interactive.md`. The steward
plans; the worker executes. Do not instruct manual ready-bead implementation in
response to broad orientation prompts.

---

"Doing work" in DDx means draining the ready queue: pick the top ready bead,
run one or more `ddx try` attempts, verify the result, and close the bead on
success or leave it available for a future eligible retry.

## Default surface: `ddx work`

```bash
ddx work
```

`ddx work` drains the queue by picking ready beads and invoking `ddx try`.
`ddx try` wraps `ddx run`, which is the single agent invocation primitive. DDx
owns queue iteration, attempt evidence, and retry policy; the upstream agent
owns provider/model routing.

Flags worth knowing:

- `--once` — pick one bead and stop (don't loop).
- `--poll-interval <dur>` — continuous worker mode; wait between
  iterations.
- `--min-power <n>` / `--max-power <n>` — requested agent power bounds.
- `--top-power` — choose a `MinPower` threshold from the agent catalog.
- `--harness <name>` / `--provider <name>` / `--model <ref>` — passthrough
  constraints only. DDx sends them unchanged and does not route on them.

## Primitive: `ddx try`

For targeted re-runs, debugging, or running a specific bead:

```bash
ddx try <bead-id>
ddx try <bead-id> --from <rev>      # base commit override
ddx try <bead-id> --no-merge        # preserve result, don't land
```

`ddx try` runs one bead attempt in an isolated git worktree. It calls `ddx run`
for the actual agent invocation.

## Pick by default, not by ID

Under normal operation, **don't specify a bead ID**. `ddx work` picks
the top ready bead based on priority + dependency satisfaction. Only
pin a specific ID when debugging (`ddx try <id>`) or
when the queue ordering would pick the wrong bead and you need to
override.

## Verify independently before closing

**Agents hallucinate successful completions.** Do not trust the
agent's self-report. Before closing a bead:

1. Run the acceptance-criteria command yourself (from the bead's
   `accept:` field). If it's `go test ./foo/...`, run that.
2. Check the resulting commit against the in-scope file list from
   the bead description. Out-of-scope files touched? Reject the
   attempt.
3. Read the commit message — does it reference the bead ID?
   (`[ddx-<id>]` or similar in the commit subject is the convention.)

If all three pass: close the bead.

## Close on success, unclaim on failure

DDx try/work outcomes form a specific taxonomy. Each outcome
maps to a concrete follow-up action:

| Outcome | Meaning | Action |
|---|---|---|
| `success` | Tests pass, AC met, commit landed | `ddx bead close <id>` |
| `already_satisfied` | No changes needed (AC was already green) | `ddx bead close <id>` |
| `no_changes` | Agent returned without producing commits and wrote `no_changes_rationale.txt` | Leave open, **unclaim** |
| `no_evidence_produced` | Agent exited without a commit or rationale | Leave open, **unclaim**, investigate harness/commit failure |
| `land_conflict` | Merge conflict on landing the result | Leave open, **unclaim** |
| `post_run_check_failed` | Tests or gate failed after landing | Leave open, **unclaim**, investigate |
| `execution_failed` | Agent subprocess errored (timeout, crash, provider error) | Leave open, **unclaim** |
| `structural_validation_failed` | Result failed structural sanity check | Leave open, **unclaim**, investigate |

`ddx work` applies these actions automatically. If you're running
`ddx try` directly, apply them manually: `ddx bead update <id>
--unclaim` to release a bead after a non-closing outcome, so another
worker can pick it up.

**Never leave a bead half-owned** — every execution either closes or
unclaims.

## Testing expectations for bead implementations

When an agent implements a bead, its output should include tests
that exercise the new code:

- **Unit tests** for logic with in-memory stubs for first-party
  collaborators. Favor stubs over mocks; mocks that assert on call
  sequences test implementation, not behavior.
- **Integration tests** against real collaborators where the cost
  is small (real git in a temp dir, real DB in a temp file, real
  HTTP via a local test server).
- **Real e2e tests** at the outermost boundary. E2e tests that mock
  the database or the network are unit tests lying about their
  scope.
- **Coverage measurement only where the project tracks it.** Don't
  introduce coverage tooling just for one bead; use what the
  project already has. Coverage is a signal, not a target.
- **Performance claims require baselines.** If the bead's AC
  mentions "faster" or "scales", the acceptance test must include:
  (a) a numeric baseline, (b) an explicit boundary (what's
  measured, what's excluded), (c) a reproducible harness.

## Anti-patterns

- **Trusting the agent's "done"**: always re-run the AC command
  yourself before closing.
- **Closing on no_changes/no_evidence**: only `success` and `already_satisfied`
  close a bead. `no_changes` requires an explicit rationale. `no_evidence_produced`
  means the agent returned without a commit or rationale — unclaim and investigate.
- **Squashing bead-attempt commits**: the per-attempt history is
  an audit trail (evidence commits, heartbeats). Use only
  `git merge --ff-only` or `--no-ff`; never squash/rebase/filter.
- **Running passthrough pins without a reason**: power-bound dispatch lets the
  agent choose an appropriate route. Use `--harness`, `--provider`, or
  `--model` only for explicit operator constraints, bug repros, or controlled
  tests.
- **Parallel workers on the same claimed bead**: the tracker
  guards against this via claim semantics, but don't try to defeat
  it — each claim represents an in-flight attempt.

## CLI reference

```bash
ddx work                                    # default queue drain
ddx work --once                             # one bead, then stop
ddx work --poll-interval 30s                # continuous worker
ddx work --min-power 10                     # request stronger attempts
ddx work --harness claude                   # passthrough constraint

ddx try <id>                                # one bead attempt
ddx try <id> --from <rev>                   # override base commit
ddx try <id> --no-merge                     # preserve iteration
```

Full flag list: `ddx work --help`, `ddx try --help`, `ddx run --help`.
"}}

Add a dependency (id depends on dep-id)

Usage:
  ddx bead dep add <id> <dep-id> [flags]

Flags:
  -h, --help   help for add

Global Flags:
      --config string              config file (default is $HOME/.ddx.yml)
      --library-base-path string   override path for DDx library location
  -v, --verbose                    verbose output

Cancel received, shutting down gracefully
Create a new bead

Usage:
  ddx bead create <title> [flags]

Flags:
      --acceptance string    Acceptance criteria
      --description string   Description
  -h, --help                 help for create
      --labels string        Comma-separated labels
      --parent string        Parent bead ID
      --priority int         Priority (0=highest, 4=lowest) (default 2)
      --set stringArray      Set custom field (key=value, repeatable)
      --type string          Bead type (task, bug, epic, chore)

Global Flags:
      --config string              config file (default is $HOME/.ddx.yml)
      --library-base-path string   override path for DDx library location
  -v, --verbose                    verbose output

data.text: The tracker CLI exposes `--parent`, which is the sane hierarchy primitive here. The prompt also asks for `dep add <child> <parent>`, but that would block every child behind an open parent. I’m checking how this repo records decompositions before I choose the least-bad auditable shape.

New fixture graded-fail-retry-spam.json + test asserts invalid_provider on turns=6 + input_tokens=0 + output_tokens=0 + had_llm_request=true + tmw=nil. 2. reclassify-invalid.py --dry-run flags the 70+ historical vidar 2026-05-11 cells as transitioning to invalid_provider. 3. After apply, those cells are excluded from vidar's pass-rate denominator. 4. Subsequent sweep with --retry-invalid re-runs them when the endpoint is back up.","claimed-at":"2026-05-11T16:57:16Z","claimed-machine":"eitri","claimed-pid":"525134","closing_commit_sha":"8a9bc87e83391e640f7885695c5b75db2ebf56e9","created_at":"2026-05-11T16:54:14.484538447Z","description":"fiz retries transient provider errors (connection refused, EOF, 5xx) internally up to ~5 times with exponential backoff. Each retry attempt counts as a turn in the matrix, inflating the turns counter even when zero useful work happened. Result: cells where the provider was completely unreachable end up with turns=6 (5 retries), output_tokens=0, terminated_mid_work=nil, but `matrixHasMeaningfulAttempt(report)` returns true because turns > 0 — bypassing the new invalid_provider classifier rule introduced in 6688326e (fizeau-e874633d).

Concrete incident: on 2026-05-11 ~15:25Z, vidar:1235 oMLX server crashed. 70 cells silently failed before detection. fiz.txt showed:
  2026/05/11 15:29:41 WARN provider error, retrying attempt=1 err="unexpected EOF"
  2026/05/11 15:29:42 WARN provider error, retrying attempt=2 err="connect: connection refused"
  ... etc through attempt=5
Each cell ended with turns=6, wall=60-200s, final_status=graded_fail. Classifier left them as plain graded_fail — i.e. "the model attempted and failed" — polluting vidar's pass-rate denominator with what was actually 100% provider unavailability.

The hole is structural: hasMeaningfulAttempt currently looks at turns/tokens/tool_calls > 0. fiz's retry counter is one of the inputs to that signal, but a retry isn't a meaningful attempt — it's the absence of one.

PROPOSED FIX

Add a stronger "no real attempt" signal that distinguishes retry-spam from real turns. Two complementary options:

A. **fiz records retry attempts separately** from successful llm.request/response pairs. The session jsonl would carry an `llm.retry` event (or annotate llm.request with an `attempt` field). The matrix runner already reads the session jsonl in readSessionSignalsFromCell — it can extend the count to distinguish "had_completed_response" from "had_request". Then: graded_fail + !had_completed_response + had_request → invalid_provider, regardless of turns count.

B. **Token-based fallback in the existing classifier**: even if turns > 0, if input_tokens=0 AND output_tokens=0 AND had_llm_request=true AND tmw=nil, classify as invalid_provider. The intuition: a real turn consumes prompt tokens (input_tokens > 0 from session.end usage). All-zero token counts means no completed round-trip ever happened. Cheaper than (A) but a bit more heuristic.

In-scope files for Part B (simpler, ships first):
- cmd/bench/matrix_invalid.go — extend the case "graded_fail" branch with the token-based check before the !hasAttempt branch.
- cmd/bench/matrix_invalid_test.go — fixture: graded-fail-retry-spam.json with turns=6, input_tokens=0, output_tokens=0, had_llm_request=true, tmw=nil → expects invalid_provider.
- scripts/benchmark/reclassify-invalid.py — mirror.
- Run reclassifier; expect ~70+ historical vidar cells from 2026-05-11 to flip invalid_setup/empty → invalid_provider.

In-scope for Part A (deeper, file as separate bead if simple fix isn't enough):
- internal/core/loop.go — distinguish "request attempted but provider error retried" from "completed request/response pair" in event emission. Add an `attempt` field on llm.request or a new event type.
- scripts/benchmark/harbor_agent.py FizeauAgent.parse_telemetry — count completed responses (not just requests) when populating turns/tokens fields.
- cmd/bench/matrix.go readSessionSignalsFromCell — return n_completed_responses alongside the existing tmw/had_llm_request signals.

ACCEPTANCE IS A COMMAND

1. go test ./cmd/bench/... -run TestClassifyMatrixInvalid passes with the new fixture graded-fail-retry-spam.json asserting invalid_provider.
2. .venv-report/bin/python scripts/benchmark/reclassify-invalid.py --dry-run flags the cells from the 2026-05-11 vidar incident (70+ cells) as transitioning to invalid_provider.
3. Apply pass leaves those cells excluded from vidar's pass-rate denominator and tagged for --retry-invalid reruns.

WHY THIS MATTERS

Without this, every server-side outage (oMLX crash, vLLM hang, network blip) gets attributed to model quality instead of provider availability. Pass@k denominators include hundreds of cells where the model never actually got to try. The defense-in-depth piece (fizeau-72d18004 consecutive-failure halt in the matrix runner) catches this at sweep-execution time, but classification matters even retroactively for accurate reporting.","events_attachment":"fizeau-25c4aea8/events.jsonl","execute-loop-heartbeat-at":"2026-05-11T16:57:16.748363613Z","id":"fizeau-25c4aea8","issue_type":"bug","labels":["area:benchmark","kind:reliability","kind:classifier"],"owner":"erik","parent":"fizeau-1a5353cd","priority":1,"schema_version":1,"session_id":"eb-e6a6d72f","status":"closed","title":"bench: classifier misses retry-spam cells (turns inflated by fiz internal retries on connection errors)","updated_at":"2026-05-11T17:04:37.498768948Z"}

After root ListModels, ResolveRoute, and RouteStatus consume the unified enriched model snapshot, legacy duplicate paths should not remain as accidental alternate sources of truth. The known legacy paths include root `service_models.go` inline model discovery/enrichment helpers and service routing live provider entry/probe helpers that bypass `modelregistry.ModelSnapshot`. Why this matters: see parent epic. The 2026-05-10 sindri-llamacpp incident wasted 119 cells (~93 min) on a `reasoning: low` parameter the llama-server provider rejects. The same hour, sindri-vllm wasted multiple cells on a hung model engine where /v1/models responded but /v1/chat/completions never streamed tokens. A single curl-to-completion check per lane catches all these classes (plus auth failures, missing-model 404s, connection-refused, wrong content-type) at sweep startup, before any matrix runner spawns.

Behavior:

- Fire after `Resolved tasks:` is printed, before the per-lane "starting" markers.
- For each lane in the resolved plan: build a JSON body using the lane's `fizeau_env.FIZEAU_MODEL` and the lane's sampling block (temperature, top_p, top_k, reasoning when present). POST to `<base_url>/chat/completions` with `max_tokens: 1` and a 30s timeout.
- Pass condition: the response is JSON-parseable and `.choices[0].message.content` is a non-null string (even an empty string is OK — the model started decoding).
- Fail condition: any non-2xx, timeout, malformed JSON, or missing message content. Print the lane id, base_url, model id, sampling params attempted, and the response body (truncated to 1KB).
- Skip preflight for managed-cloud lanes that have always-on SLAs (openrouter, openai, anthropic) — these are well-known and a preflight just burns quota. Identify by checking the resource group's `provider_type` against an allow-list (vllm, llama-server, omlx, lmstudio, rapid-mlx, ollama, lucebox).

In-scope files:
- scripts/benchmark/run_terminalbench_2_1_sweep.sh — add preflight_lane() function + per-lane invocation loop right before the existing per-lane plan output.
- scripts/benchmark/terminalbench-2-1-sweep.yaml — no schema change needed; the existing `fizeau_env` and `sampling` blocks are sufficient.

Out-of-scope (file separate beads):

4. The cloud lane skip works: `scripts/benchmark/run_terminalbench_2_1_sweep.sh --phase full --lanes openrouter-qwen36 --dry-run` does not call openrouter and prints `[preflight] openrouter-qwen36 SKIPPED (managed cloud lane)`.
